meta.certificationName: AWS Certified Machine Learning Engineer – Associate
meta.title: AWS-MLE-4: Data Preparation, Serving Choices, and Operational Readiness

domains.name: Deployment and Orchestration of ML Workflows

questions.stem: An implementation review is underway. An ML platform owner wants users to have only the permissions required to build, train, and deploy approved models. Which security principle should guide the IAM design?

options.text: Rely on network ACLs instead of IAM policies
Use one shared root account for all data scientists

options.explanation: **Apply least-privilege IAM permissions** is correct because AWS recommends granting only the permissions required for each role and workflow. Granting full admin access or relying solely on account-wide or network controls weakens security and governance.

questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AIAmazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/security_iam_id-based-policy-examples.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A machine learning team needs to explore, clean, and transform tabular training data with a visual workflow before training in Amazon SageMaker. Which SageMaker capability BEST fits this requirement?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler** is correct because it is designed for data preparation and feature engineering workflows before training. **Model Monitor** is for post-deployment monitoring, **Inference Recommender** benchmarks inference options, and **Ground Truth** focuses on data labeling.

questions.reference: [Prepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AIPrepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **govern model versions with approval states before promotion**. Which service or feature is the best fit?

options.explanation: It stores versioned model packages and approval status for governed promotion workflows
It is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets
It is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario
It does not act as the central online/offline feature repository for SageMaker ML features

questions.reference: [SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A team needs versioned records of trained models, approval states, lineage, and a controlled handoff from experimentation to production. Which SageMaker feature addresses this need MOST directly?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Registry** is correct because it manages model versions, approval status, lineage, and deployment governance. **Neo** optimizes models for target hardware, **EventBridge Scheduler** schedules events, and **EMR Studio** is unrelated to model lifecycle governance.

questions.reference: [Model Registration Deployment with Model Registry - Amazon SageMaker AIModel Registration Deployment with Model Registry - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem:
A model receives occasional very large payloads and can take several minutes to produce each prediction.

Which hosting option is BEST?

options.explanation: Asynchronous inference is designed for large payloads or long processing times.

options.text: Real-time endpoint with the smallest instance
options.explanation: Small real-time endpoints are a poor fit for long-running requests.

options.text: Batch Transform only if clients need immediate responses
options.explanation: Batch Transform is offline rather than request/response.

options.text: Serverless inference with a strict sub-second expectation
options.explanation: Serverless is not the preferred choice for long-running asynchronous jobs.

questions.reference: [Asynchronous inference - Amazon SageMaker AIAsynchronous inference - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/async-inference.html)

questions.stem:
A team is reviewing its approach. A data science team needs a managed way to run feature engineering and evaluation code before training begins.

Which SageMaker capability is the BEST fit?

options.explanation: Batch Transform is for offline inference, not pre-training processing.
SageMaker Processing is the managed service for preprocessing and evaluation jobs.
Automatic model tuning searches hyperparameters rather than running preprocessing code.
Model Monitor is for post-deployment or ongoing monitoring.

questions.reference: [Data transformation workloads with SageMaker Processing - Amazon SageMaker AIData transformation workloads with SageMaker Processing - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/processing-job.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **store reusable ML features for both training and online inference**. Which service or feature is the best fit?

options.explanation: It is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps
It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring
It does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs
It provides managed online and offline feature storage to reduce training-serving skew

questions.reference: [SageMaker Feature Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)

questions.stem:
A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A responsible AI review requires bias analysis and explainability for model behavior before and once the model is deployed.

Which SageMaker feature BEST addresses that need?

options.explanation: AWS Config records resource configuration changes rather than ML explainability.
Batch Transform performs offline predictions, not bias analysis.
SageMaker Clarify provides bias detection and explainability workflows.
Athena queries data but does not provide model bias explanations.

questions.reference: [Pre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AIPre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-detect-data-bias.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **SageMaker Clarify**?

options.explanation: Teams should choose it when they need to analyze bias and explainability in ML workflows
It is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets
It is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario
It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

questions.reference: [SageMaker Clarify overview](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-fairness-and-explainability.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **AWS Glue**?

options.explanation: It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring
It does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs
It is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps
Teams should choose it when they need to crawl schemas and run serverless ETL over raw training data

questions.reference: [AWS Glue concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **SageMaker Experiments**?

options.explanation: Teams should choose it when they need to compare training runs, parameters, metrics, and artifacts
It is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps
It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring
It is not the main SageMaker capability for bias and explainability analysis

questions.reference: [SageMaker Experiments](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/experiments.html)

questions.stem: An implementation review is underway. A platform team wants a repeatable ML workflow that chains preprocessing, training, evaluation, approval, and deployment steps with lineage and auditability. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Pipelines** is correct because it is purpose-built for orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows with SageMaker integrations and lineage. **Step Functions** can orchestrate general workflows but is not the purpose-built ML pipeline service described here. **Kinesis** and **AppConfig** do not provide ML pipeline orchestration.

options.text: AWS Step Functions Express Workflows only

questions.reference: [Pipelines - Amazon SageMaker AIPipelines - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **SageMaker Model Registry**?

options.explanation: It does not act as the central online/offline feature repository for SageMaker ML features
It is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario
It is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets
Teams should choose it when they need to govern model versions with approval states before promotion

questions.reference: [SageMaker Model Registry](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-registry.html)

questions.stem:
A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A responsible AI review requires bias analysis and explainability for model behavior before and once the model is deployed.

Which SageMaker feature BEST addresses that need?

options.explanation: Athena queries data but does not provide model bias explanations.
SageMaker Clarify provides bias detection and explainability workflows.
AWS Config records resource configuration changes rather than ML explainability.
Batch Transform performs offline predictions, not bias analysis.

questions.reference: [Pre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AIPre-training Data Bias - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-detect-data-bias.html)

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A data scientist needs a managed way to run distributed training jobs, store model artifacts in Amazon S3, and track the resulting output for later deployment. Which SageMaker capability should be used?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker training jobs** are correct because they provide managed training infrastructure and produce model artifacts that can be deployed later. **CloudFront**, **Storage Gateway**, and **Route 53** do not perform model training.

questions.reference: [Model training - Amazon SageMaker AIModel training - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/train-model.html)

questions.stem:
A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A model receives occasional very large payloads and can take several minutes to produce each prediction.

Which hosting option is BEST?

options.text: Serverless inference with a strict sub-second expectation
options.explanation: Serverless is not the preferred choice for long-running asynchronous jobs.

options.text: Real-time endpoint with the smallest instance
options.explanation: Small real-time endpoints are a poor fit for long-running requests.
Asynchronous inference is designed for large payloads or long processing times.

options.text: Batch Transform only if clients need immediate responses
options.explanation: Batch Transform is offline rather than request/response.

questions.stem:
A team is reviewing its approach. A production team wants to detect data drift and model quality issues after a model is deployed.

Which managed SageMaker capability is the BEST fit?

options.explanation: Processing is for standalone jobs rather than deployment monitoring.
Model Monitor is designed for ongoing checks such as data and model quality drift.
Glue Studio is for data integration, not deployed model monitoring.
Notebooks are development tools, not managed drift monitors.

questions.reference: [Data and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AIData and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

questions.stem:
A team needs to compare training runs, parameters, and metrics from multiple SageMaker experiments.

Which feature is MOST directly designed for that purpose?

options.explanation: SageMaker Experiments tracks and compares model-development runs.
Batch Transform is for inference on batch data.
EventBridge routes events rather than tracking experiments.
Lambda layers are unrelated to experiment tracking.

questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker Experiments in Studio Classic - Amazon SageMaker AIAmazon SageMaker Experiments in Studio Classic - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/experiments.html)

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A platform team wants a repeatable ML workflow that chains preprocessing, training, evaluation, approval, and deployment steps with lineage and auditability. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.text: AWS Step Functions Express Workflows only

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Pipelines** is correct because it is purpose-built for orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows with SageMaker integrations and lineage. **Step Functions** can orchestrate general workflows but is not the purpose-built ML pipeline service described here. **Kinesis** and **AppConfig** do not provide ML pipeline orchestration.

questions.reference: [Pipelines - Amazon SageMaker AIPipelines - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.stem:
Which service or feature is most closely associated with the following need?

- Goal: crawl schemas and run serverless ETL over raw training data
- Constraint: use the managed vendor capability when possible

What should the team choose?

options.explanation: It is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps
It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring
It provides crawlers, ETL jobs, and a metadata catalog for serverless data preparation
It does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs

questions.reference: [AWS Glue concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/components-overview.html)

questions.stem: A pilot project exposed a design mistake. A machine learning team needs to explore, clean, and transform tabular training data with a visual workflow before training in Amazon SageMaker. Which SageMaker capability BEST fits this requirement?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler** is correct because it is designed for data preparation and feature engineering workflows before training. **Model Monitor** is for post-deployment monitoring, **Inference Recommender** benchmarks inference options, and **Ground Truth** focuses on data labeling.

questions.reference: [Prepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AIPrepare ML Data with Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/data-wrangler.html)

questions.stem: An ML platform owner wants users to have only the permissions required to build, train, and deploy approved models. Which security principle should guide the IAM design?

options.text: Rely on network ACLs instead of IAM policies
Use one shared root account for all data scientists

options.explanation: **Apply least-privilege IAM permissions** is correct because AWS recommends granting only the permissions required for each role and workflow. Granting full admin access or relying solely on account-wide or network controls weakens security and governance.

questions.reference: [Amazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AIAmazon SageMaker AI identity-based policy examples - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/security_iam_id-based-policy-examples.html)

questions.stem: A model must handle unpredictable request spikes with minimal infrastructure management and no need to keep idle instances running. Which deployment pattern is MOST appropriate?

options.text: A large multi-AZ self-managed EC2 Auto Scaling group

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Serverless Inference** is correct because it fits intermittent or spiky workloads without managing always-on instances. **Batch transform** is offline inference, and the EC2 or notebook options add unnecessary management overhead for this requirement.

options.text: Notebook instances exposed through a load balancer

questions.reference: [Deploy models for inference - Amazon SageMaker AIDeploy models for inference - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/deploy-model.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A platform team wants a repeatable ML workflow that chains preprocessing, training, evaluation, approval, and deployment steps with lineage and auditability. Which AWS service is purpose-built for this?

options.text: AWS Step Functions Express Workflows only

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Pipelines** is correct because it is purpose-built for orchestrating end-to-end ML workflows with SageMaker integrations and lineage. **Step Functions** can orchestrate general workflows but is not the purpose-built ML pipeline service described here. **Kinesis** and **AppConfig** do not provide ML pipeline orchestration.

questions.reference: [Pipelines - Amazon SageMaker AIPipelines - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.stem: A project is at risk because engineers selected the wrong tool. Which option best addresses this requirement: **analyze bias and explainability in ML workflows**?

options.explanation: It is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario
It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring
It is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets
It provides bias detection and explainability capabilities for SageMaker ML systems

questions.reference: [SageMaker Clarify overview](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-fairness-and-explainability.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **analyze bias and explainability in ML workflows**. Which service or feature is the best fit?

options.explanation: It is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets
It provides bias detection and explainability capabilities for SageMaker ML systems
It is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario
It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring

questions.reference: [SageMaker Clarify overview](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/clarify-fairness-and-explainability.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. A production team wants automated checks for data quality drift and model quality issues after a model is deployed. Which SageMaker feature should they enable?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor** is correct because it continuously monitors deployed models for data quality, bias, feature attribution, and model quality signals. **JumpStart** provides solution starters, **DataBrew** prepares data, and **QuickSight Q** is unrelated to ML endpoint monitoring.

questions.reference: [Data and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AIData and model quality monitoring with Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

questions.stem:
A practitioner needs the most reliable approach. An ML engineer must join and transform large raw datasets before model training, while maintaining a metadata catalog for repeatable ETL.

Which AWS service is the BEST fit?

options.explanation: Model Registry governs model versions, not ETL.
CodeDeploy is not the primary service for analytical ETL.
AWS Glue is AWS’s managed ETL and catalog service for data preparation workflows.
CloudWatch is for monitoring.

questions.reference: [What is AWS Glue? - AWS GlueWhat is AWS Glue? - AWS Glue](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/glue/latest/dg/what-is-glue.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **search hyperparameter ranges against an objective metric**. Which service or feature is the best fit?

options.explanation: It is the managed hyperparameter optimization feature in SageMaker
It does not act as the central online/offline feature repository for SageMaker ML features
It is not the primary AWS service for serverless ETL cataloging in this ML-preparation scenario
It does not provide versioned model approval and governance for promotion

questions.reference: [SageMaker automatic model tuning](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/automatic-model-tuning.html)

questions.stem:
Which service or feature is most closely associated with the following need?

- Goal: detect drift and quality issues once the model is deployed
- Constraint: use the managed vendor capability when possible

What should the team choose?

options.explanation: It serves a different ML lifecycle need than managed preprocessing jobs
It profiles baselines and monitors deployed models for data and model quality issues
It is not the best fit for offline batch scoring of large datasets
It is not the main SageMaker capability for bias and explainability analysis

questions.reference: [SageMaker Model Monitor](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/model-monitor.html)

questions.stem: A team needs to **orchestrate processing, training, evaluation, and registration steps**. Which service or feature is the best fit?

options.explanation: It does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs
It does not act as the central online/offline feature repository for SageMaker ML features
It is the managed orchestration layer for repeatable end-to-end ML workflows in SageMaker
It serves a different ML lifecycle need than managed preprocessing jobs

questions.reference: [SageMaker Pipelines](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/pipelines.html)

questions.stem: A practitioner is reviewing platform options. When should they choose **SageMaker Feature Store**?

options.explanation: It is not the primary orchestration service for end-to-end SageMaker ML steps
It does not primarily provide experiment tracking across model-development runs
It is not the AWS feature intended for post-deployment drift and quality monitoring
Teams should choose it when they need to store reusable ML features for both training and online inference

questions.reference: [SageMaker Feature Store](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)

questions.stem: A company wants to compute features once, reuse them across multiple models, and serve the same feature definitions for both training and online inference. Which service should the team use?

options.explanation: **Amazon SageMaker Feature Store** is correct because it centralizes feature definitions and supports both offline and online feature access. **S3 Glacier**, **Athena prepared statements**, and **CloudWatch Logs Insights** do not provide managed ML feature storage for training and serving reuse.

questions.reference: [Create, store, and share features with Feature Store - Amazon SageMaker AICreate, store, and share features with Feature Store - Amazon SageMaker AI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sagemaker/latest/dg/feature-store.html)
